codeReview.summary: Solid, well-structured implementation of signed-in turn persistence spanning backend service, REST endpoints, frontend hook integration, and API helpers. The backend service properly verifies session ownership before all read/write operations, uses Firestore subcollections for turn storage, and correctly handles title updates with validation. The frontend hook saves transcript state after each `turn_complete` event (signed-in only), loads saved turns on session reopen, generates titles from the first user message, and properly guards all persistence behind `sessionMode === 'persisted'` checks. Guest sessions are entirely excluded from persistence writes by design — they have no server-side session record, so any attempt to save/load returns 404. Tests cover the critical paths with 12 well-targeted cases. The FakeFirestoreClient was cleanly extended with subcollection and merge-write support. All linting, type checking, and build checks passed.

issues.description: The save/title endpoints accept `body: dict` instead of a Pydantic model. This means FastAPI won't validate the request body schema, and malformed payloads (missing keys, wrong types) will silently pass through to the service layer. The existing artifact persistence endpoint in the same file follows the same `body: dict` pattern, so this is consistent with the codebase convention, but worth noting as tech debt — a typed Pydantic model would give automatic 422 validation and OpenAPI documentation.

The `turns` parameter is typed as `list[dict[str, Any]]` with no validation on individual turn structure. A client could send turns with missing `role` or `content` keys, or inject arbitrary fields, and they'd be stored verbatim in Firestore. The frontend `messagesToPersistedTurns` does enforce the correct shape on the client side, but the backend doesn't validate. This is low-risk since all writes require auth and ownership, but backend validation would be more robust.

The `persistCurrentTurns` function uses `savePendingRef` as a simple boolean guard to avoid concurrent saves, but if a save fails, the next `turn_complete` will retry with the full current state (which is correct since each save replaces the full turn list). However, there's a subtle race: if the user produces turns faster than the save roundtrip, some intermediate states are lost — only the state at the time of save initiation is persisted. This is acceptable because the next successful `turn_complete` will persist the full accumulated state, but it means the last persisted state could be slightly stale if the app crashes between turn_complete events.

The `generateSessionTitle` function generates titles client-side by truncating the first user message. The feature description says 'AI-generated titles' but this is actually a simple substring truncation. This is pragmatic for a hackathon-posture project, and the test (VAL-SESSION-004) validates title persistence regardless of generation method. The title generation approach is simple but adequate.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The turn persistence endpoints (PUT/GET /sessions/{id}/turns, PATCH /sessions/{id}/title) follow the same `body: dict` pattern used by the existing artifact persistence endpoints. This pattern of using untyped dict bodies instead of Pydantic models is becoming a recurring convention across all brainstorm REST endpoints. If this is intentional for hackathon speed, it could be documented in AGENTS.md as a known convention; if not, it should be flagged as tech debt to standardize.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: src/app/api/v1/endpoints/brainstorm.py lines 374, 419, 446 all use `body: dict`. The existing artifact save endpoint at line ~310 also uses `body: dict`.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The Firestore data model now uses subcollections: each brainstorm session document has a `turns` subcollection with a single `latest` document that holds the full turn state. This subcollection pattern is not documented in the architecture library file or AGENTS.md. Future workers touching persistence should know about this structure to avoid creating conflicting patterns.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: src/app/services/brainstorm_turn_persistence.py defines BRAINSTORM_TURNS_SUBCOLLECTION='turns' and BRAINSTORM_TURNS_DOCUMENT_ID='latest'. The architecture.md mentions 'Firestore stores brainstorm session metadata, saved transcript/turn state' but doesn't describe the subcollection shape.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The voidpilot-knowledge.md file still says 'All artifacts are held in React state with no server-side storage.' This is now outdated — both artifacts and transcript turns have server-side persistence for signed-in sessions. The knowledge base should be updated to reflect the current persistence model.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: .factory/library/voidpilot-knowledge.md under 'Frontend Artifact Content Format' states: 'State: All artifacts are held in React state (Map<string, BrainstormArtifact>) with no server-side storage.'

summary: The implementation is solid and passes review. All declared assertions (VAL-SESSION-002 through VAL-SESSION-008) are properly covered: signed-in sessions save transcript state after each turn and restore it on reopen, voice transcripts are preserved as readable text, sessions get titles (client-generated from first user message), reopening appends to the same session via full-state replacement, and guest sessions are completely excluded from persistence. The backend enforces ownership on all operations, the frontend properly gates persistence behind session mode checks, and 12 backend tests cover the critical paths including cross-user access denial. Non-blocking issues are minor: untyped dict request bodies (consistent with existing convention), no backend turn schema validation (mitigated by auth+ownership), a potential stale-state window during rapid turns (mitigated by full-state replacement), and client-side title generation being simpler than 'AI-generated' implies. None of these are blocking.
